title: Changing Data (UPDATE)
description: Fix a typo or change a user's setting.

slides.title: Oops, a typo! 🤦‍♂️
slides.content:
# Fixing Mistakes

What if a user misspells their email address during sign-up? Or what if their subscription status changes from 'Free' to 'Pro'?

We don't inject a massive brand-new duplicate row... we simply edit the existing one.

slides.title: The UPDATE Command 🔧
slides.content:
## Making changes

To modify existing data, we use the **`UPDATE`** keyword, followed by the table name.

Then, we use the **`SET`** keyword to declare exactly what column should be changed to what new value.

`UPDATE users SET status = 'Pro';`

quizData.question: What does the `SET` keyword do in an update command?

options.text: It sets the database on fire
It literally defines which columns will receive the new altered data
It sets up the table for deletion

quizData.explanation: UPDATE targets the table, but SET defines the actual mechanical change you are pushing to the columns.

slides.title: Updating Multiple Columns 🥞
slides.content:
## Two birds, one stone

You can easily update multiple columns at the exact same time by separating them with commas.

`UPDATE users SET status = 'Pro', account_balance = 50.00;`

Notice that we didn't write SET twice! Just a simple comma.

quizData.question: Which properly formatted query correctly updates both a user's role and email instantly?

options.text: UPDATE users SET role = 'Admin' AND email = 'hq@gmail.com';
UPDATE users SET role = 'Admin' SET email = 'hq@gmail.com';

quizData.explanation: Commas cleanly separate multiple column assignments within a single SET clause.
